title: runner.sh : deal with bash 'set -e'
body:
Release trigger script failed[1] because the entire script
runs under 'set -e'; so a 'grep -- -dev' that finds no
results will cause a nonzero exit status and hence the
entire script to fail. Work around that.

 [1] https://cirrus-ci.com/task/4541290882793472

Signed-off-by: Ed Santiago <santiago@redhat.com>

comments.body: @cevich PTAL, this should address the failure you linked in scrum today

[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *cevich*, *edsantiago* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/OWNERS)~~ [edsantiago] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

@edsantiago now that you've fully experienced the pain of testing this code in the intended CI context...perhaps you understand why I'm not happy having these checks in this repo.

What do you think about my idea of moving them into a script in `c/automation`, so the task in this repo. would simply pull the external code down and run it?

Advantages:
* We can leverage CI in `c/automation` to exercise the release code vs a mock-release of podman
* During podman release testing (i.e. after a tag-push), if something goes south, we can address it by modifying code in `c/automation` to fix it. Thus avoiding the need to push a new podman tag only to fix the release-test code.

@cevich that seems like a high-effort solution to a low-risk problem, but I could definitely see it as a good idea if someone decides to add new `release` tests. That would be the ideal time to make this change, IMO, but if you feel like tackling it sooner I will be glad to review your changes.

> if someone decides to add new release tests

@edsantiago yes, that was my thinking as well...and why it's just a "TODO" comment.  Though I'm also thinking bigger, in having more automation involved in (or running) the release process.  My Jira card is about exploring these ideas more fully with the group, to see what level of automation makes sense.
